items.notes: Universal greeting, works in all situations, informal and formal
examples: Hei, miten menee? — Hi, how's it going?

items.notes: Informal greeting, used with friends and family
examples: Moi moi! — Hi hi! (casual double greeting)

items.notes: Slightly more neutral than 'moi', literally means 'healthy'
examples: Terve, tuletko mukaan? — Hello, are you coming along?

items.notes: Used until about noon. 'Huomenta' alone is more casual.
examples: Hyvää huomenta! Nukuitko hyvin? — Good morning! Did you sleep well?

items.notes: Formal daytime greeting, used in shops and offices
examples: Hyvää päivää, kuinka voin auttaa? — Good day, how can I help?

items.notes: Used from late afternoon onwards
examples: Hyvää iltaa kaikille! — Good evening everyone!

items.notes: Said when going to bed or parting late at night
examples: Hyvää yötä, nuku hyvin! — Good night, sleep well!

items.notes: Formal goodbye, literally 'until we see again'
examples: Näkemiin ja kiitos! — Goodbye and thank you!

items.notes: Casual goodbye, very common in everyday speech
examples: Hei hei, nähdään huomenna! — Bye bye, see you tomorrow!

items.notes: The most important word in Finnish. Used very frequently.
examples: Kiitos paljon avusta! — Thank you very much for the help!

items.english: you're welcome / please / here you go
items.notes: Multipurpose: response to 'kiitos', handing something over, or 'please'
examples: Ole hyvä, tässä on kahvisi. — Here you go, here's your coffee.

items.notes: Used for apologies and to get attention politely
examples: Anteeksi, missä on asema? — Excuse me, where is the station?

items.notes: Formal 'yes'. In casual speech, 'joo' is more common.
examples: Kyllä, olen suomalainen. — Yes, I am Finnish.

items.notes: Also the negative verb stem in Finnish. 'Ei' conjugates: en, et, ei, emme, ette, eivät.
examples: Ei kiitos, en halua. — No thanks, I don't want any.

items.notes: Casual 'yes', very common in spoken Finnish
examples: Joo, mä tulen! — Yeah, I'm coming!

items.notes: 'Paljon' means 'a lot / much'
examples: Kiitos paljon, tämä oli todella mukavaa! — Thank you very much, this was really nice!

items.notes: Used to welcome guests. Compound: terve (healthy) + tulo (arrival).
examples: Tervetuloa Suomeen! — Welcome to Finland!

items.notes: Casual way to ask how someone is. Literally 'how goes'.
examples: Hei, miten menee? — Hi, how's it going?

items.notes: Common response to 'miten menee'. Adverb form of 'hyvä' (good).
examples: Kiitos, hyvin menee! — Thanks, it's going well!

items.notes: Adverb form of 'huono' (bad). Honest Finns might actually say this!
examples: Menee aika huonosti tänään. — It's going pretty badly today.

items.notes: Literally 'fun to meet'. Used when meeting someone for the first time.
examples: Hauska tavata, olen Lilian. — Nice to meet you, I'm Lilian.

items.english: nice to get to know you
items.notes: Slightly more formal than 'hauska tavata'. 'Tutustua' = to get acquainted.
examples: Hauska tutustua! Mistä olet kotoisin? — Nice to get to know you! Where are you from?

items.notes: 'Nimi' = name, '-ni' = my (possessive suffix). Casual: 'Mä olen...'
examples: Nimeni on Lilian. — My name is Lilian.

items.notes: 'Mikä' = what, 'sinun' = your, 'nimesi' = your name. Informal.
examples: Mikä sinun nimesi on? — What is your name?

items.notes: Passive form of 'nähdä' (to see), literally 'it will be seen'. Very common casual goodbye.
examples: Nähdään huomenna! — See you tomorrow!

items.notes: Casual and friendly goodbye, common in spoken Finnish
examples: Heippa, pidä hauskaa! — Bye, have fun!

items.notes: Partitive form of 'hyvä'. Used in greetings: 'hyvää huomenta/päivää/iltaa'.
examples: Hyvää viikonloppua! — Have a good weekend!

items.notes: Casual short form of 'hyvää huomenta'. Very common.
examples: Huomenta! Kahvia? — Morning! Coffee?
